questions.text: In a triangle, if side AB is longer than side AC, then which angle is greater?
questions.explanation: In a triangle, the angle opposite to the longer side is greater. Since AB > AC, angle opposite to AB (which is angle ACB) is greater than angle opposite to AC.

questions.text: If in triangle PQR, angle P is greater than angle Q, which side is longer?
questions.explanation: The side opposite to the greater angle is longer. Since angle P > angle Q, side opposite to angle P (which is QR) is longer than side opposite to angle Q.

questions.text: Which of the following inequalities represents the triangle inequality theorem?

options.a: Sum of any two sides is less than the third side
options.b: Sum of any two sides is equal to the third side
options.c: Sum of any two sides is greater than the third side
options.d: Difference of any two sides is greater than the third side

questions.explanation: The triangle inequality theorem states that the sum of any two sides of a triangle is greater than the third side.

questions.text: In triangle XYZ, if XY = 7 cm, YZ = 10 cm, what is the possible range for side XZ?
questions.explanation: By triangle inequality, difference of two sides < third side < sum of two sides. So, |10 - 7| < XZ < 10 + 7 => 3 < XZ < 17.

questions.text: If an exterior angle of a triangle is 120°, what can be said about the sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles?
questions.explanation: Exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles.

questions.text: In triangle ABC, if AB = AC, then which of the following is true?
questions.explanation: In an isosceles triangle, sides equal imply opposite angles are equal.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT possible for the sides of a triangle?
questions.explanation: Sum of any two sides must be greater than the third. 5 + 7 = 12, which is not greater than 12, so these cannot form a triangle.

questions.text: If in triangle DEF, side DE is the longest, which angle is the greatest?
questions.explanation: The angle opposite the longest side is the greatest. DE is opposite to angle F.

questions.text: In triangle ABC, if angle A = 90°, which side is the longest?
questions.explanation: In a right-angled triangle, the hypotenuse (side opposite the right angle) is the longest side. Angle A is 90°, so BC is the hypotenuse.

questions.text: If the difference of two sides of a triangle is 4 cm and the third side is 9 cm, which of the following is true?

options.a: 4 cm < 9 cm < sum of the other two sides
options.c: 9 cm > sum of the other two sides
options.d: Difference of two sides > third side

questions.explanation: Difference of two sides < third side < sum of two sides. So, 4 < 9 < sum of other two sides.

questions.text: In triangle PQR, if angle P = 40°, angle Q = 60°, what is the measure of angle R?
questions.explanation: Sum of angles in a triangle is 180°. So, angle R = 180° - 40° - 60° = 80°.

questions.text: If in triangle ABC, side BC = 8 cm, side AC = 5 cm, and side AB = 3 cm, which side is the longest?
questions.explanation: Side BC = 8 cm is the longest among 3 cm, 5 cm, and 8 cm.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is true?

options.a: Exterior angle is less than each non-adjacent interior angle
options.b: Exterior angle is equal to each non-adjacent interior angle
options.c: Exterior angle is greater than each non-adjacent interior angle
options.d: Exterior angle is equal to the adjacent interior angle

questions.explanation: An exterior angle of a triangle is greater than each of the two non-adjacent interior angles.

questions.text: In triangle XYZ, if XY = YZ, then which of the following is true?
questions.explanation: Sides equal imply opposite angles equal. Since XY = YZ, angles opposite these sides (Z and X respectively) are equal.

questions.text: In triangle ABC, if AB + BC = AC, what can be said about the triangle?

options.c: Points A, B, and C are collinear

questions.explanation: If sum of two sides equals the third side, the points are collinear and do not form a triangle.

questions.text: In triangle PQR, if side PQ = 6 cm, side PR = 10 cm, and angle Q > angle R, which side is longer?
questions.explanation: Angle opposite longer side is greater. Since angle Q > angle R, side opposite Q (PR) should be longer than side opposite R (PQ). Given PR > PQ, so side QR opposite to angle P must satisfy triangle inequalities. But since angle Q > angle R, side opposite Q (PR) is longer, so side QR is between PQ and PR.

questions.text: If in triangle ABC, angle B is an exterior angle equal to 110°, and angle C = 40°, find angle A.
questions.explanation: Exterior angle B = sum of two non-adjacent interior angles A + C. So, 110° = A + 40°, hence A = 70°. But angle B is exterior to angle B, so the adjacent interior angle is 70°, so angle A = 30°.

questions.text: In triangle XYZ, if XY = 8 cm, YZ = 5 cm, and XZ = 3 cm, which inequality is true?
questions.explanation: Sum of any two sides is greater than the third side. 8 + 5 = 13 > 3.

questions.text: In triangle ABC, if angle A = 50°, angle B = 60°, which side is the longest?
questions.explanation: Largest angle is 70° (180° - 50° - 60° = 70°), opposite side is longest. Angle C = 70°, opposite side AB.

questions.text: In triangle PQR, if side PQ = 7 cm, side QR = 10 cm, and side PR = x cm, what is the range of x?
questions.explanation: By triangle inequality, difference of two sides < third side < sum of two sides. So, |10 - 7| < x < 10 + 7 => 3 < x < 17.

questions.text: A triangular plot has sides of lengths 50 m, 70 m, and x m. If the angle opposite to the side of length x is the greatest angle of the triangle, which of the following could be the value of x?
questions.explanation: The angle opposite the longest side is the greatest. Since the angle opposite to side x is greatest, x must be greater than 70 m and 50 m. Among the options, only 80 m is greater than 70 m.

questions.text: In a triangle, the sides are 12 cm, 15 cm and 28 cm. Is this a valid triangle? If not, why?

options.c: Yes, because 28 is the longest side

questions.explanation: Sum of any two sides must be greater than the third side. Here, 12 + 15 = 27 which is less than 28, so it is not a valid triangle.

questions.text: A carpenter is making a triangular frame with sides 24 cm, 32 cm and 40 cm. Which angle is the largest?
questions.explanation: The angle opposite the longest side is the greatest. Here, 40 cm is the longest side, so the angle opposite it is the largest.

questions.text: A triangle has two sides measuring 9 cm and 15 cm. Which of the following could be the length of the third side?
questions.explanation: The third side must be less than the sum and greater than the difference of the two sides. So, 15 - 9 < x < 15 + 9 => 6 < x < 24. Among options, only 5 cm is less than 6, so it cannot be the third side. Hence, 5 cm is invalid, others are invalid too except those between 6 and 24. So correct answer is 5 cm as a possible length? Actually question asks which could be the length. So 5 cm is invalid. So options are tricky. The correct third side length must be greater than 6 and less than 24. So 25 cm and 30 cm are invalid, 5 cm invalid, 24 cm equals upper limit so valid. So correct answer is 24 cm.

questions.text: In a triangle ABC, if side AB = 8 cm, side AC = 6 cm and angle B is 70°, which side is longer: BC or AC?
questions.explanation: Angle opposite longer side is greater. Since angle B is 70°, side AC opposite to angle B is 6 cm. Side BC is opposite to angle A. Without angle A, but since AB=8 cm, BC is likely longer than AC. By triangle inequality and angle-side relation, BC > AC.

questions.text: A triangular field has sides 40 m, 55 m, and 70 m. What is the range of possible lengths for a fence that can be placed along the third side if one side is missing?
questions.explanation: If one side is missing, it must satisfy triangle inequality with the other two sides. So, difference < third side < sum. For sides 40 m and 55 m, third side x satisfies 55 - 40 < x < 55 + 40 => 15 < x < 95.

questions.text: In triangle PQR, the exterior angle at vertex Q is 110°. If the interior angle at vertex P is 50°, what is the measure of the interior angle at vertex R?
questions.explanation: Exterior angle at Q = sum of non-adjacent interior angles P and R. So, 110° = 50° + angle R => angle R = 60°.

questions.text: A triangle has sides 13 cm, 24 cm, and 25 cm. Which angle is the smallest?
questions.explanation: The smallest angle is opposite the shortest side. Here, 13 cm is shortest side, so angle opposite 13 cm side is smallest.

questions.text: A triangular signboard has sides measuring 10 m, 16 m, and 20 m. If the angle opposite the 20 m side is x, which of the following is true?

options.c: x is equal to the angle opposite 16 m side

questions.explanation: Angle opposite the longest side is the largest. Since 20 m is longest side, angle x opposite it is largest.

questions.text: In triangle XYZ, side XY = 7 cm, side YZ = 10 cm, and side XZ = 12 cm. Which inequality is true?
questions.explanation: Check triangle inequality: 7 + 10 = 17 > 12, so XY + YZ > XZ is true.

questions.text: In triangle ABC, AB = 7 cm, AC = 10 cm, and angle B = 40°. If angle C is greater than angle B, which of the following is the possible length of BC?
questions.explanation: Since angle C > angle B, the side opposite angle C (AB) should be greater than the side opposite angle B (AC). Given AB = 7 cm and AC = 10 cm, this is contradictory unless BC is longer than both. Using triangle inequality and angle-side relations, BC must be greater than 10 cm, so 12 cm is possible.

questions.text: In triangle PQR, side PQ = 8 cm, side PR = 6 cm, and angle QPR = 50°. If an exterior angle at Q is 110°, find the range of possible lengths of side QR.
questions.explanation: Exterior angle at Q = 110° > angle QPR = 50°, so angle Q > 60°. Using triangle inequality, sum of any two sides > third side and difference < third side, side QR must be greater than difference (8-6=2) and less than sum (8+6=14). Considering angle relations, side QR must be >4 cm. Hence between 4 cm and 14 cm.

questions.text: In triangle XYZ, XY = 9 cm, YZ = 12 cm, and angle X = 45°. If the exterior angle at Z is 130°, which side is the longest?
questions.explanation: Exterior angle at Z = 130° > sum of interior opposite angles X + Y. Since angle X = 45°, angle Y < 85°. Side opposite larger angle is longer. Since exterior angle at Z is large, angle Z is small, so side XZ opposite angle Y or X must be longest. Using triangle inequality and angle-side relations, XZ is longest.

questions.text: In triangle DEF, DE = 15 cm, EF = 20 cm, and angle D = 60°. If angle E is greater than angle F, what is the possible range of length of side DF?
questions.explanation: Since angle E > angle F, side opposite E (DF) > side opposite F (DE = 15 cm). Using triangle inequality, DF < DE + EF = 35 cm and DF > EF - DE = 5 cm. But since DF > 15 cm, the range is between 15 cm and 35 cm. Among options, 10 cm to 25 cm fits the closest with the given constraints.

questions.text: In triangle ABC, AB = 13 cm, AC = 15 cm, and BC = 24 cm. If angle B is extended to form an exterior angle of 120°, which interior angle is the smallest?
questions.explanation: Exterior angle at B = 120°, so interior angle B = 60°. Using triangle inequality and side-angle relations, side opposite smallest angle is shortest side. Shortest side is AB = 13 cm opposite angle C or AC = 15 cm opposite angle B. Since angle B = 60°, angle C is smallest.

questions.text: In triangle MNO, MN = 10 cm, NO = 14 cm, and MO = 18 cm. If the exterior angle at N is 140°, prove which side is the longest and justify using inequalities.

options.a: MO is longest because it is opposite the greatest angle
options.b: NO is longest because it is opposite the smallest angle
options.c: MN is longest because it is opposite the exterior angle
options.d: Cannot determine longest side from given data

questions.explanation: Exterior angle at N = 140° > sum of interior opposite angles M + O. Since MO = 18 cm is the largest side, it is opposite the largest angle. By triangle inequality and angle-side relations, MO is longest.

questions.text: In triangle ABC, AB = 7 cm, AC = 9 cm and BC = 12 cm. Find the largest angle of the triangle and justify your answer.
questions.correct_answer: Angle opposite to the longest side BC is the largest, so angle A is the largest.
accepted_forms: Angle opposite to BC is largest
Angle opposite to the longest side BC is largest
questions.explanation: In a triangle, the angle opposite the longest side is the greatest. Here, BC = 12 cm is the longest side, so angle A opposite BC is the largest angle.

questions.text: In triangle PQR, angle P = 40° and angle Q = 65°. Which side is the longest? Justify your answer.
questions.correct_answer: Side PR is the longest because it is opposite the greatest angle Q, which is 65°.
questions.explanation: The side opposite the greater angle is longer. Since angle Q = 65° is the greatest angle, side PR opposite angle Q is the longest.

questions.text: Prove that in any triangle, the sum of any two sides is greater than the third side using a suitable construction.
questions.correct_answer: By joining the points and using triangle inequality, AB + BC > AC (or similar), proving the sum of any two sides is greater than the third side.
accepted_forms: Sum of any two sides is greater than the third side
AB + BC > AC, BC + AC > AB, AC + AB > BC
questions.explanation: Using the triangle inequality theorem and construction, it can be shown that sum of any two sides of a triangle is always greater than the third side.

questions.text: In triangle XYZ, XY = 8 cm, YZ = 5 cm and XZ = 12 cm. Is triangle XYZ possible? Justify your answer using the triangle inequality theorem.
questions.correct_answer: No, triangle XYZ is not possible because XY + YZ = 13 cm which is greater than XZ = 12 cm but YZ + XZ = 17 cm > XY and XY + XZ = 20 cm > YZ, so the sides satisfy triangle inequality, so triangle is possible.
questions.explanation: Check if sum of any two sides is greater than the third. Here, all sums satisfy the inequality, so triangle is possible.

questions.text: In triangle DEF, DE = 10 cm, EF = 7 cm and DF = 4 cm. Find the difference between the two longer sides and check if it is less than the shortest side.
questions.correct_answer: Difference between DE and EF is 3 cm which is less than DF = 4 cm, so the triangle inequality holds.
questions.explanation: Difference of longer sides DE and EF is 10 - 7 = 3 cm which is less than the shortest side DF = 4 cm, satisfying the triangle inequality.

questions.text: In triangle ABC, an exterior angle at vertex C is 110°. If the interior opposite angles are 40° and x°, find x and verify the exterior angle property.
questions.correct_answer: x = 70°; exterior angle 110° = 40° + 70°, so exterior angle is greater than each opposite interior angle.
questions.explanation: Exterior angle equals sum of two opposite interior angles. Given 110° = 40° + x°, so x = 70°. Exterior angle is greater than each opposite interior angle.

questions.text: Prove that the side opposite to the greater angle in a triangle is longer than the side opposite to the smaller angle.
questions.correct_answer: By comparing angles and opposite sides using triangle properties, it is proved that side opposite greater angle is longer than side opposite smaller angle.
accepted_forms: Side opposite greater angle is longer
Proof of side opposite greater angle is longer
Side opposite greater angle > side opposite smaller angle
questions.explanation: Using properties of triangles and inequalities, one can prove that the side opposite the greater angle is longer than the side opposite the smaller angle.

questions.text: In triangle MNO, MN = 6 cm, NO = 9 cm and angle M = 30°. Find the possible range of length for side MO using triangle inequalities.
accepted_forms: MO is between 3 cm and 15 cm
questions.explanation: Using triangle inequalities: MO + MN > NO => MO > 3 cm; MO + NO > MN => MO > -3 cm (always true); MN + NO > MO => MO < 15 cm. So, 3 cm < MO < 15 cm.

questions.text: In triangle PQR, angle PQR is an exterior angle equal to 120°. If the two non-adjacent interior angles are 50° and x°, find x and verify the exterior angle theorem.
questions.correct_answer: x = 70°; exterior angle 120° = 50° + 70°, verifying exterior angle theorem.
questions.explanation: Exterior angle equals sum of two opposite interior angles. Given 120° = 50° + x°, so x = 70°. This verifies the exterior angle theorem.

questions.text: Using the triangle inequality theorem, determine whether a triangle is possible with sides 15 cm, 25 cm and 40 cm. Justify your answer.
questions.correct_answer: No, triangle is not possible because 15 + 25 = 40 cm, which is not greater than the third side 40 cm.
questions.explanation: Sum of two sides must be strictly greater than the third side. Here, 15 + 25 = 40, which equals the third side, so triangle is not possible.

questions.case_text: Rahul is designing a triangular garden where the lengths of two sides are 15 m and 22 m. He wants to ensure the third side allows the garden to have a valid triangular shape. He also wants to place a lamp post opposite the longest side and a bench opposite the smallest angle. He needs to decide the possible length of the third side and understand the relationship between sides and angles in his garden design.

subparts.text: What is the range of possible lengths for the third side of the triangular garden?
subparts.explanation: By the triangle inequality, the third side must be greater than the difference and less than the sum of the other two sides: 22 - 15 = 7 m < third side < 22 + 15 = 37 m.

subparts.text: If the third side is 30 m, which side is the longest and which angle is the smallest?

options.a: Longest side = 30 m, smallest angle opposite 15 m side
options.b: Longest side = 30 m, smallest angle opposite 22 m side
options.c: Longest side = 22 m, smallest angle opposite 15 m side
options.d: Longest side = 15 m, smallest angle opposite 30 m side

subparts.explanation: With sides 15 m, 22 m, and 30 m, 30 m is longest. The smallest angle is opposite the shortest side, which is 15 m, so smallest angle is opposite 15 m side.

subparts.text: Calculate whether the triangle with sides 15 m, 22 m, and 30 m is valid using the triangle inequality.
subparts.explanation: Sum of any two sides is greater than the third: 15+22=37>30, 15+30=45>22, 22+30=52>15. Hence, the triangle is valid.

questions.case_text: An engineer is constructing a triangular support frame for a bridge. The frame has two sides measuring 40 m and 55 m. The engineer wants to determine the possible lengths for the third side to ensure stability. Additionally, the engineer needs to understand how the angles opposite these sides compare and verify if an exterior angle is greater than the non-adjacent interior angles for safety checks.

subparts.text: What is the possible range of lengths for the third side of the triangular frame?
subparts.explanation: By triangle inequality, third side must be greater than difference (55-40=15 m) and less than sum (55+40=95 m).

subparts.text: If the third side is 50 m, which angle is the greatest?
subparts.explanation: The greatest angle is opposite the longest side, which is 55 m.

subparts.text: If an exterior angle of the triangle measures 120°, what can be said about the measures of the two non-adjacent interior angles?
subparts.explanation: The exterior angle is greater than each of the two non-adjacent interior angles.
